headline: Dress Code Enforcement Bars 1,200 Women from Government Offices
context: A 2025 survey by the All Women's Action Society documented 1,200 cases where women were denied entry to government offices for dress code violations including wearing shorts, sleeveless tops, or short skirts.

cards.big: 1,200 women were turned away from government offices for their clothing in 2024.
cards.sub: No uniform national dress code exists. Standards vary by building, officer, and state.

cards.big: Article 8(2) prohibits gender discrimination in public services.
cards.sub: Dress codes applied predominantly to women — 94% of those turned away — constitute de facto discrimination.

cards.big: 28 different dress code standards exist across federal and state offices.
cards.sub: Some require knee-length, others ankle-length. Compliance is impossible without advance knowledge of local rules.

cards.big: 34% of women turned away were attempting to access essential services.
cards.sub: Birth registrations, land transfers, and court appearances were among denied services.

cards.big: Denying essential services based on hemline length is not modesty. It is gatekeeping.
Government offices serve the public. If dress codes are necessary, they should be uniform, published, reasonable, and never bar access to essential services.
